text_content: As was also found out by the breeding experiment with foxes, the lower stress environment lowers the cortisol stress hormone which causes other neurochemical changes that increase docility and sociability. As with most animals, remove them from the stressful life of every day being a fight for survival for a few generations and you get a creature that has become so human adapted that their personalities can take a massive u-turn from the usual standoffishness and wariness of their wild counterparts. Adding _the_ dog breed that by default sees everything as a friend has fast tracked this in cheetahs.

text_content: I had a Maine Coon. His name was Cujo. He weighed 20lbs and was the ruler of my other five cats I had at the time. He would play a game with me involving dry cat food. I would ask him if he was ready to play, at which point, he would take his position on the kitchen floor and wait for me to serve each nugget of Iams dry cat food, by sliding it toward him. He would swipe his paw, catching each piece before it reached it's destiny, and ate it. He loved this game and would continue to play until my knees got sore. I sure do miss him.

text_content:
The Turkish breeds (yeah, they have regular cats too) are the Anatolian Angora and Van. Basically there's only two primary breeds, the Anatolian and the Angora,(the longer haired version of the Anatolian). The Van Cat is generally (hey, they are cats, they mix up a lot) just a color variety of the other two. The Van markings are (generally) a color patch on the head and (sometimes including the ears and a face mask) and tail, with one or more patches of color on the rear flank and occasionally also on the shoulder.

The Van's color patches can be a solid color, tabby, or (in females) Calico. The standard (short hair) Van and Anatolian cats have a short, soft and dense multi layer fur that is water resistant (they like water and swimming!), and the Angora are basically just a longer haired variety of the other two. The Angora also seems to have a really long tail, but they are not Persian. All three varieties have a rather small and wedge shaped head much like the original Siamese (before they bred them into sphinx like freaks) with fairly high cheek bones. They have longer rear legs than most breeds, and are excellent jumpers. When you look at them in profile, the butt is higher than the shoulders. The males are generally very muscular and are almost dog like when they walk. (Sometimes) They have great temperaments if raised properly, and are affectionate and even protective of their people and family members. I had a male (about twenty pounds, they ARE densely built) that charged down a full grown Rottweiler that was barking at my wife and I. He definitely wasn't happy about that. Ran him right out of the yard! 

They Turkish breeds are rather rare in the US, (as purebreds) but I've noticed that there are a lot of cats running around that have a lot of Turkish traits, which is good, as it can only improve their genetics. The few purebreds out there are fairly pricey, but the mixed ones are just like any other cats, so if you find one that has those traits, you should consider cooperating with the Cat Distribution System, and take one (or two, etc) home! They are really great Cats! 

Edit: the primary color is White! And (unlike most) the all white males often have Blue eyes, but are NOT deaf! So if you find a white male cat with blue eyes that's not deaf, they are almost certainly Anatolian (Turkish, etc.) They also are more prone than most breeds to have different colored eyes, usually blue and amber, or green, especially the females. It's another fairly distinctive trait in the Turkish breeds.
Not all of the males have blue eyes, but I think that most do.🐈🐈🐈🐈🐈

text_content: I remember when I was growing up, we found 2 kittens. We kept them for a couple of months, but one of them was normal with a slight mean streak and the other seemed to have mental issues. The normal one would give us the killer look,  but never attacked us. At night, she would just walk up and down on us while we slept in our beds like she was prowling and protecting us. We were just kids then. We always had cats, but they were different and we never knew what type of kittens we had gotten. They were loyal and liked to play along with cuddles, but we had to get rid of them because they would not use the litter box no matter how hard we tried. I’m looking at this video, and the Savannah cats look exactly like them and I am certain they were Savannah. Same spots, slender bodies, and big in size for cats under a year old.

It how far Mondo got in few days shows how much these cats needs space to move around. Their living space should not be one room or small cage. They need space to get that exercise. 



If you do clips like this you should also bring out the negatives that comes with owning cat like this. How they are bread example. It is not without problems. text_content: Just so everyone is aware, yes cougars/pumas/mountain lions can be owned in the in most US states with a permit (some don't require one). And yes by definition cougars are not considered big cats (like lion and tigers) because they are lacking the hyoid bone adaptation that allows them to roar (and some other bone structure that are different). But this does not make them ok to own. They are still wild animals that are incredibly strong and can weigh 75-160 lbs. they don't typically attack humans but there have been cases of them killing people. Cougars typically have a territory size of 50-150 sq miles which would be hard to provide in a suburban home. Let's not normalize keeping large predatory animals as pets

text_content: A good friend of mine served in the Peace Corps in Kenya for 2 years, including one full year in the bush.  He told me that all of the big cats, including lions will generally leave humans alone, EXCEPT leopards.  The reason is that all of the other cats hunt only 4 legged prey.  But the leopard is the only big cat that regularly hunts baboons, which move on 2 legs, and that similarity to us puts us on their menu.

The cheetah’s slowly stalked him as he rode the mower, and he’d get off it about every two minutes and throw a ball.  They’d all chase the ball. text_content: Am showing this to my wife. I give Princesses Myu catnip about every 2 1/2 months and she digs it. Myu is my baby. I rescued her at what my vet determined was about 11 days old. Until that day I was honestly not a cat person. But that day I determined to be her papa and learned a whole lotta shit from my vet. I became a mother cat for a kitty. It was awesome! And when I learned my princess could have catnip I gave it a go, she loved it! My wife FREAKED out. I backed it up with research and verified professionals but this did not deter my wife from believing I was introducing our beloved 王女さま (princess as we live in Japan) to the devils lettuce. Maybe it’ll change her mind, maybe it won’t. Either way I know big cats and my princess both love to roll with this shit the same way👍🏻

text_content:
The reason why Cheetahs and panthers meow and purr is because they share a common ancestor with domestic cats, as evident by their family placement in the Felinae family, as proposed to the Panthorae family with the big cats that can roar.

These cats are physically incapable of roaring, so they're just little murder cuties! <3

video_title: They are just bigger cats🐱#cat #cats #fyp #funny
text_content: Actually just like big cats they generally do not ever meow past a few months old.  Meowing is usually only a signal used for young cats to get the attention of their parents when still not independent.  However due to a still unknown reason cats, including big cats, will meow at their humans.  In fact most cats raised by humans tend to continue to display many child like behaviors around those humans for their entire life.

video_title: They are just bigger cats🐱#cat #cats #fyp #funny
text_content:
I've actually had a cheetah in my lap.  Was a teen, and they had their cheetah ambassadors  Kiam from the Wildlife Safari at the mall.  I got my turn to sit by Kiam, and she decided my lap was the preferred spot, and kept turning around to look at me.  The handler was really confused by all this as I'm the only one she'd shown interest in, much less this much.  Wildlife Safari still has a Kiam, kind of like Seaworld has Shamu, they just give the name to a new one over time.  Funny coincidence about that encounter with Kiam. My future wife was in the crowd and remembers seeing me and how Kiam was so interested in me.  
It was a great experience, though I have to say she was really warm, and her fur was really rough as well. I'd still pet a cheetah on my lap, totally worth it.

text_content: I'll never forget the first Maine Coon I ever met: Fred.  Fred laid in the show window of a hardware store in Hillsborough, New Hampshire when the sun was out.  At first I thought it was a fake stuffed animal, a HUGE catlike toy.  But then Fred yawned.  OMG!  I went in and spoke with the old man who ran the store. He told me all about his buddy Fred and the Maine Coon breed. What sweet gentle giants they are!

text_content: When I was a child, I lived with my aunt and uncle, and she would feed this cat that was hidden under a office trailer in the middle of an abandoned car dealership. I remember she feed him for a long time and then finally he was comfortable enough to come close and she brought him home. He was edgy, aggressive, and loud. None of the other cats wanted to go near him because of how big he was. Found out from the vet he was a crossbreed with Coon and some other breed I cannot recall. He seemed to have never been well socialized, as he was cautious around everyone and every animal we had and couldn't meow, but he tried and this little squeak like a mouse would come from him. Named him Squeaky. Through many scratches, and bites, and hissing,  he finally came trust me and was my biggest buddy for years; sleeping in my bed, joining me for breakfast (Where he knew I'd give him some of my milk and bacon), and guarding my room from other cats or dogs. He died around the age of 13 or so. RIP little buddy.

text_content: My Maine Coon lived to be 19.5 years old, broke my heart when he passed. They don't need to be huge to be a great pet. Aileron was 18 pounds in his prime. He could stand with back feet on the floor and get his nose above the counter top. He always had his place at the dinner table. He would sit and watch us eat and would never beg. One night we were having better cheddar hotdogs (stuffed with cheese) and when I bit into mine it squirted melted cheese into Aileron's face. He calmly cleaned his face at the table and never complained. Don't under estimate the female side of the breed. They are smaller but have every attribute of these fine feline companions.

text_content: We adopted a 32 pound Maine coon mix when his previous family moved to a complex that didn't allow pets. Nolan Ryan (aka Nollie) quickly turned into a Mama's boy who shared the bed while hubby worked night shift and stretched out on me in the recliner - with his back paws touching my feet and his front paws hugging my neck. We couldn't leave meals unattended because Nollie would stretch his front paws onto the dining table and scoot plates, pizza boxes, whatever, to the edge and onto the floor. He loved to go outside on a leash and roll in the catnip patch I planted, but would dash into my arms whenever a truck passed by (but cars didn't scare him, go figure. 🤷) Nollie was such a sweet, toddler sized, big baby of a cat and we still miss him dearly some 20 years after he crossed the 🌈🌉. RIP, "Papa Bear Nollie"

text_content:
I own one. In fact, my pfp is of him. He's turning 18 this year had him since I was a toddler. More loyal than my dog. A great heater in the winter. Super calm and chill. Low maintenance. He gets along with all people. Never bit or scratched anyone.  He used to be quite big. Somewhere in the mid 20lbs to slightly over 30lbs in his prime. And it was mostly muscle, not much fat. He used to be super stocky. But now in his old age, he can't jump anymore and has lost quite a bit of weight. If he wants to lay on the couch with me I have to pick him up, and if he wants to be on the ground I have to put him down (I do it without complaint, hes worth it). Other than that, he is still pretty healthy though, will follow me around the house like my shadow. I can't speak for all main coon owners. But, at least from my experience growing up with one, they are a joy to have around the house for the family. Never once had an issue with him.

The only thing you gotta watch out for is the long hair. It gets everywhere and needs to be brushed regularly.

text_content: We have one and a Norwegian Forest cat which is thought to be the bigger ancestral version of a Coone. I'm 6 ft tall and I can spread my arms out as wide as they go while holding him and he's still bigger than that. He is the most enormous cat I have ever personally seen up close. Also, one of the coolest cats I've ever come in contact with. My son found him in a forest (NW WA), somehow abandoned by his mother as a kitten and all alone and I realized very quickly as I had previously done cat rescue that this was NOT a domesticated species of cat. He acts like a dog, follows my son everywhere and patrols our property like it's his job. I've seen him stand up to deers and anything else that comes our way. These are truly amazing cats, both breeds. You better buy a lot of food though, and oftentimes they're not meant to be kept only inside. They really enjoy going outside. Our Norwegian I call our mini Bob for bobcat, He's about 22 lb right now and not done growing and he makes our Maine Coon look small in comparison.

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content: Oh, look, they actually are friend-shaped friends.

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content:
Cheetahs do suffer from anxiety a lot. So much so that even in the wild some just stop hunting and die of starvation.
In captivity the anxiety can get even worse.
So giving them a dog companion, especially something like a Golden Retriever whose main goal in life is to make friends and adopt everything and anything can be really beneficial to a Cheetah.
So yes, these big Cats, the largest breed of the Small Cats family actually, do need a puppy of their own.

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content: Cheetahs look to other cheetahs for social cues so a group of cheetahs can quickly go on an anxiety spiral of doom, especially in captivity. That's why you put them with golden retrievers who are perpetually unbothered by everything and believes absolutely everyone is their best friend.

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content: The purring calms me down ALWAYS.

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content: Fun fact, cheetahs kept in captivity often need emotional support dogs. It's incredibly common

video_title: Why Cheetahs Are Basically Turbocharged House Cats #animals #cheetah #cute
text_content: Cheetahs are actually more closely related to the small cat family (Felinae), rather than being in the big cat family (pantherinae) this is why they don't posses the same roar that lions, tigers, and leopards posses.

text_content:
For all those whom are concerned and wondering about why this young man has a tiger is, because he rescued Romeo from being sold to the circus. Romeo, the tiger I do believe will be given sanctuary once he grows bigger into his adult stage and be with his own kind. He is not a pet, but this young man considers him family as well as Romeo. Romeo thinks of him as a parental figure and family. The day they have to part will be devastating, but once he adjusts to his new environment it will be for the best. Of course this young man will visit when he can and the reunions will be amazing. Obviously, owning tigers are illegal unless you are known as a rescuer, who rehabilitates and gives sanctuary to certain types of animals for a period of time then transfers them to other qualified well known animal sanctuaries that have the money to do so. 

All in said, please support your local, well-known, and national sanctuaries and rescue places, because it helps their mission to feed, supply medical care, daily care, rescue more animals, advertise, and raise awareness world-wide even local. Thank you!

text_content:
For anyone who didn't know, this is actually very cat-like behavior even with house cats. Most people don't do it with their cats, anecdotally speaking at least, but with two of the three cats I've had I've basically played "tag" with them. If they seem like they got the zoomies or just feel like playing, I'll sometimes chase them down the hallway in the upstairs of our house. I stop short of the bedroom at the end of the hall while they'd dart in and usually around a corner for "safety." I'd give them a second, and then either peek in or make a slightly exaggerated amount of noise as I start to run away and before I know it, cat's gunning it back at me. I run into my room, turn around, chase him back and repeat. Then there's the more common game that can be mixed in of peering around corners at each other and them closing the distance in between your peeks.

My cat did scare the shit out of me one time during a rare first floor play session running between the living room and kitchen. I just wanted to do a round or two, so after I chased him to the kitchen, I walked part of the way to the stairs and then kinda went up them quickly. I hear something and look behind me and the mfer is gunning for me up the fucking stairs and so I'm like whoa there and go faster and he kept chasing me all the way into my room and I'm like this mfer is gonna cut me holy shit. 

Obviously I died after that.

text_content:
Animals are actually more violent and prone to attacking when blind because it's hard to tell what's actually a threat or not, so the fact that the tiger is so calm and gentle shows the amount of love and patience this family has given this sweet panther!



Edit: Thanks for the love to those who liked, but this will be my one and only edit because apparently I have to be a science teacher to get the... "not so intelligent" people out of my replies, so here it goes. 
A panther is not an animal. 
The term "panther" is a genus, or class of animals, describing big cats. A "Black panther" is not a specific animal. It's a member of the panthera genus with melanism. Melanism makes you black, hence the name "Black panther". 
It's a description, not an animal. A black lion is a black panther, a black tiger is a black panther, in the same way that a black jaguar is a black panther. My using of the word panther there was correct and scientifically accurate. It is not my job to change the way I referred to an animal because it's not comfortable for you. I simply just didn't wanna refer to him as a tiger back to back in the comment i made, but apparently it triggered the people who think they're educated, yet call a panther a specific animal.
I hope you learned something, have a good day.

Edit 2: I guess I lied about no more edits. I wanted to thank all the other smart people who came to defend me because I work and I rlly don't have time to challenge a lack of braincells or the refusal to learn full time so thanks for the support. I also appreciate every one who did learn something and were kind in the comments :) 
My intention was only ever to give fun facts because I love animals so I'm glad I get to share that love with yall.

text_content:
We live in large subdivision located in fair size town in the Idaho mountains. We’ve had mountain lions found bedding under blue spruce trees in front yards in the middle of winter .  One took two cats in one night which could be extremely dangerous for young children and small adults who walk past within four feet of that same tree .   I’ve mentioned this to the neighbors and suggested that the branches at ground level should be removed to at least five feet above the ground but nothing has happened so I’m going to the city .
We’ve had similar situations in nearby subdivisions but instead of cats they were going after full grown elk , dragging them underneath a tree ten feet from my friends driveway and feeding on the elk for most of the winter and because of the deep snow we’re completely unseen .Next spring he trimmed the branches up to eight feet high. They have similar videos of a lion coming up to the picture windows eyeing their two dogs .

text_content: I'm puzzled why people in the comments are criticizing the security guard, when in fact, he was trying to save numerous lives. The tragic incident of the escaped lion highlights the importance of responsible animal ownership. While it's tempting to blame the security guard for shooting the lion, I firmly believe he made the right call to protect human lives. The real culprit, in my opinion, is the lion's owner, who kept a wild animal in captivity. By doing so, he not only put his own life at risk but also the lives of those around him. The lion's eventual escape and tragic demise were inevitable consequences of its unnatural confinement. The security guard's actions, though unfortunate, were necessary to prevent further harm to the community. Ultimately, this incident serves as a reminder of the dangers of keeping wild animals in domestic environments.

text_content: I understand that cheetahs can be raised from cubs and effectively turned into 100-pound house cats; that's a notable exception among wild animals, and big cats in particular.  I have never heard of anyone even attempting to raise a jaguar as a "pet."  Jaguars are big, strong, powerful cats -- pound for pound, one of the strongest, and considerably stronger than the typical mountain lion/puma.  The girl shown in this video appears kind of smallish, but she's still built like a tank, with jaws that could crush the skull of most mammals.  It appears the guy in the video has a great relationship with his cat, but I would never seriously consider a jaguar as any sort of animal to keep in captivity.

text_content: Back in the mid 80's a friend of mine went to a Barnum and Bailey Circus in Atlanta Georgia and he bought a pit pass so he could wander around behind the scenes. He came on some train cars with animal cages on them and he noticed that they had lions and tigers in cages together, but the jaguars were in cages only with other jaguars. My buddy asked the animal keeper why that was, and he told my buddy "If you put a lion or a tiger in a cage with a jaguar, it will kill the lion or the tiger."

text_content: My one story about jaguars:  I was at a nature park in Southern Mexico, and came upon an enclosure labeled "jaguar."  It was a large enclosure with the natural, thick, local limestone rainforest habitat, but there wasn't any part of the enclosure that was blocked from view.  I looked and looked but couldn't see any jaguars in there, so I figured I'd had the misfortune to come when the enclosure was empty for cleaning, maintenance, etc.  I was just about to walk away when the black jaguar who had been lounging on a tree limb stretched, yawned, and repositioned itself.  It was literally right in front of me, not 20 feet away, and I had been looking straight at it for at least a minute.  But it blended so well with the broken sunlight and random shadow pattern of the jungle canopy that I hadn't been able to see it until it moved--it had been completely invisible to my eyes and brain, even though I had been actively looking for it.  If we'd have been in the bush, I could have walked right into that enormous murder cat and never have seen it, and obviously, it could have killed me before I had any idea I was even in any danger.  It was an amazing demonstration of how effective their camouflage is in their natural environment, and a sobering demonstration of something a local jungle guide told me:  if you walk into jaguar territory and walk out alive, it's because the jaguar decided to allow you to live that day.

text_content:
There was this film crew that went to the Amazon to try and film a nature documentary about a legendary local jaguar, and they set up a bunch of trail cams all over the place, and set up hides and hiked through the jungle for ages. The locals said they probably would never see him, even they only ever saw him once. But they keep going out and sitting in tents and stuff for months. Nothing. No jaguars at all. They found lots of tracks with huge paw prints, but never actually saw a jaguar.

So they pack up and fly home. And they review the trail cam footage, see if anything is usable and…the freakin giant jaguar was stalking THEM the entire god damn time.

I wish I could remember the title of the documentary, but I think it was either on BBC or PBS in the US a couple years ago. Google isn’t helping.

He said he knew all the sounds of the animals, and on the 3rd day the entire jungle went deadly quiet, and he knew that meant he was being stalked by a Jaguar. I was seriously driving at midnight and it was the scariest story I ever heard. The guy said his last night trying to sleep was absolutely terrifying because he was just sitting in the dark. The guy knew everything about the Amazon and the way he described it was chilling. For some reason the cat never attacked him and he had no idea why. Cray cray.

text_content:
This is completely inaccurate.  She (Vika) never thought she was raising a cat or kitten, nor did she find her on the side of the road.  

She always knew Luna was a panther.  It was the zoo that reached out to Vika because of her expertise with wild animals and she agreed to raise Luna.  Venza, is the Rottweiler that Luna was raised with.  She also owns a white owl, 2 snakes, 5 large cockroaches and feeds roe deer, hares, and a fox and horses that wander onto the property.  I’ve followed Vika and her partner, Vova for years.  Check them out at “Luna the Pantera”.  If you’re going to provide content please be factual.

text_content: What Jon says here confirms what I have heard from other sources.  Setting aside the problem of how to ethically purchase a healthy cheetah cub, the biggest challenge with maintaining a healthy cheetah in captivity is properly sourcing their diet.  If cheetahs do not receive the proper mix of proteins and other nutrients, they will experience various chronic health problems and experience shortened life spans.  From a personality and instinct perspective, cheetahs are the easiest big cat to adapt to a human environment.  Far easier in fact than tigers, lions, leopards, jaguars or pumas, who are potentially very dangerous even when hand-raised from infancy by their humans.  If socialized from cubs to interact with humans, cheetahs will bond and imprint on their human family, and interact like an overgrown house cat.  That said, they instinctually want to mark their territory -- and a 75- to 125-pount cheetah can produce a lot of urine.  They also want to claw and chew inanimate objects.  These are not "indoor" cats; they need to be kept outside a human home, with access to appropriate open space for exercise and play.  At best, having a pet cheetah is an expensive luxury that requires a significant input of cash, time and effort that is beyond the reach of most human beings to provide.

text_content:
Cheetahs are from the same divergent branch of the feline family tree as house cats. That’s why they both purr. Cheetahs also aren’t ambush predators, and are too small to see humans as food, so they’re very docile. Finally, like most cats, they know how to poop in a sand box.

The downside is that to own a healthy cheetah you have to own a ton of land where it can run for exercise. That’s the real constraint (beyond laws) on widespread adoption of cheetahs as dog-sized cat pets.

text_content:
No, cheetahs don't "know that they'd lose that fight". A cheetah could definitely kill a human. Those who think cheetahs are weak, fail to realize how much force is required to accelerate to 70 mph faster than an F-1 car. Cheetahs can accomplish that astonishing feat of strength, because the muscles needed to leap forward are over-sized and over-charged with fast twitch muscles, and every other part of the body is as lightweight as possible.

The reasons why a cheetah would lose against a heavier cat or hyena are that 1) they have more muscles in total, and 2) their more balanced muscle distribution allows multiple fighting techniques.

Against a human, though, none of that would apply. Simply grabbing us and kicking us in the gut would result in complete disembowelment in a fraction of a second. Our last sight would be our guts airborne above the savannah (exaggeration for effect).

Fortunately for us, cheetah instincts are adapted to deal with other animals, where the rule is that slow animals have good and dangerous defenses, while fast animals don't. Cheetah specialization is to avoid dealing with defenses, and instead dealing with flight. Quite simply, the fact that humans are pathetically slow is a major reason why they don't see us as prey. Another reason is that cheetahs are solitary. Any serious injury is a death sentence, even with a technical "victory". If a creature isn't known to be safe prey, a cheetah won't risk it.

Oh, and the "blunt" claws of cheetahs is a huge exaggetation. Their front claws are fully retractable, and as sharp as on any cat. Their hind claws are only partially retractable, leaving them somewhat blunter, but still much sharper than on most animals, and they're extremely sturdy, to handle the immense force of a cheetah's kick.

text_content: For those of you who don't know, you'll almost NEVER get hunted by a cheetah in the wild since they're quite timid and afraid of humans, and even though a fight between a human and a cheetah would most likely go in the cheetah's favor, they don't take the chance. They really are just big cats.

video_title: Cheetah Core #animalshorts #animals #cute #cheetah
text_content:
Reminder that cheetahs while not endangered, are "vulnerable."
In 1900, there were an estimated 100,000 cheetahs in the wild.  Their number stands today under 6,500.  A 94% decrease in 125 years.

video_title: Cheetah Core #animalshorts #animals #cute #cheetah
text_content:
Fun fact: unlike Lions, Tigers...and pretty much any other large-ish predatory mammal, Cheetah do not get the compulsory instinct to attack a human when their back is turned. They're more likely to start rubbing against you if they feel comfortable enough in your presence, or simply keep their distance if not.

Out of all known African predators, Cheetahs are probably the most un-suited for the environment, as they're truly a glass-cannon build: they can dish out decent enough damage, but absolutely fold under even remotely similar responses. They cannot protect their kills, and cheetah cubs are considered easy targets as the parents (knowing the innate fragility of cheetah existence) will struggle to put up much of a fight if forced to protect them.

Cheetahs are one of the few species that was already being out-competed by other predators before humans arrived on the scene and made things worse.

text_content: Fun fact: it's legal, with an exotic pet license, to own a cheetah. It's fairly simple, like a regular cat. But obviously much more expensive. They eat the equivalent of 12 chickens a day, need a ton of space to roam and run, and unless you can be there often, they need a friend like a gentle dog such as a golden retriever or border collie. Cheetah's are very socially awkward and get anxious easily. Having a fluffy friend to turn to if their owner isn't there helps them a lot.

Story!
Back in the 1960's, when owning exotic pets was legal, our local country garage, kept a pet cheetah---the customers all knew her and loved her, but, here's the fun!
One day two, out-of-town, "smart guys", in an open top car,  seeing no-one around, to ask for payment, decided to fill-up, and go!
Big mistake! Huge!  As they sped off waving cheekily, the owner said to his pet, "FETCH"
The local cops found the two, half an hour later, sittting petrified, in the front seat, with the Cheetah, sitting behind them, purring loudly.
They seemed positively relieved to be arrested!  LOL

text_content: for context, cheetahs are naturally nervous (highly alert) animals. because in the wild, they share habitats with not only animals in heavier weight class, but also have to deal with pack animals like hyenas stealing their food all the time. growing up in capativity away from their peers can exacerbate the anxiety. so adopted cheetahs usually are paired with a dog from breeds that are just a ball of positivity all the time, like retreivers and border collie etc, to help them chill out essentially. retrievers are usually selected to pair with cheetahs because both golden retriever and labrador retriever can have very similar coat colour to cheetahs and zoo keepers/animal rescuers think that helps with forming a bond quickly between the two species. but no evidence suggests that's the case. there are many cheetahs raised alongside border collies/shepards etc all grew up to be confident animals. cheetahs are also probably the easiest animal to form domestic cat-like bonds with humans among all african cats. i don't think they care about the colour and shape of their friend. they just want a friend to grow up with.

text_content:
I would be careful about having one as a pet, but the short also isn't entirely wrong. Humans were actually in the process of domesticating the cheetah up until about the 8th century. As such, they're sort of "half-domesticated", which is why they have such issues with anxiety and they still have those genes that say "yeah humans are kinda cool". They were living near people for thousands of years, until one day that stopped happening. Imagine everyone just abandoned their dogs one day and thousands of years were allowed to pass. The resulting descendants probably wouldn't be domesticated anymore, but would probably not be completely wild either as they'd still have that genetic response to humans on some level.
This is also why they get along with dogs and it calms their anxiety, another holdover from the time they were almost household pets themselves.

text_content: I'm a psychologist. I work with animals all the time. This one is easy. Big cats roar, small cats purr. Big cats have an innate "fight or flight" response to humans. Small cats do not. Cheetahs are the largest of the small cats. They may be apprehensive or frightened of you, but they won't attack you unless you threaten them. There has never been a recorded case of a cheetah attacking a human without being provoked first. And, to say they are "perfect pets" is the most ignorant thing you can say. Can they be domesticated? Yes. Should they be? No.

Fun Fact: Cheetahs are not big cats. In fact, they aren't even closely related to Big Cats (family, Panthera). Cheetahs actually sit at the small cat family (family, Felidae), which includes domestic cats and cougars. One of the main features of Big Cats is the ability to roar (exception being the Snow Leopard), and Cheetahs cannot roar. They meow or chirp. 
Now, as for the clip of the jaguar (Panthera Onca) carrying the caiman: yeah, they can do that. Jaguars are the third biggest cat in the world. They also have the most powerful bite of any big cat, topping at 1300 psi. They're all terrain apex predators in the Americas (yes, I did say Americas, cause they have been seen in the Southern United States).

text_content: I recommend gentle dominance with cats.  Most felines don’t comprehend punishment very well and usually will listen out of fear or be anti-social.  If a cat is being persistently hazardous and/ or destructive, I usually pick them up by supporting under the chest with one hand and gently picking them up by the scruff of their neck and relocating.  I try to use physical behavior only when necessary though and calling their name or yelling is usually the first resort.  I’ve met a lot of very aggressive animals in my life who were abused but if you give them space and occasional treats they’ll usually open up to you on their own, just don’t rush it and move slow and in sight if it’s a particularly antisocial animal.  Be calm and relaxed but also prepared to pull away if any defensive aggression is displayed towards you.  Dominance discipline is usually not needed because the right behavior will display dominance.  When you provide food, set boundaries, and occupy your territory, a smaller animal can usually sense you’re the boss unless you act too enabling

text_content:
I used to have a bear cub. Yea sounds crazy, and it was crazy. We never kept him in the house, he was free to go anywhere whenever he pleased. 
He came to our door everynight, and stayed near our house to visit us most times we were outside. We assumed his mother had died because we never saw her. So we fed him. It got the the point where we would pet him and play with him like he was a dog. 
Months pass and he had become family to us. After a few days of not seeing him, we found out he had been shot and killed through facebook.
The man who killed him saw him come near our house and thought he was like any other bear. He wasnt a pet. He was a medium size male black bear. 
I dont remember what we used to call him. I was a kid so I know I had to have given him a name at some point. All I remember is that he LOVED honey buns.
Wild animals can be tamed, not domesticated - and nobody should take them from their natural environment. Even small friendly interactions can impact their wellbeing. ❤❤

I had a cat once that heard me throwing up in the toilet, I heard him calling to me running through the house to find me, he came into the bathroom, walked slowly up to me then reached out and placed his paw on me until it was over. 

We were close before this, but after that, I never loved an animal more.

Recently, I almost ran over a kitten on a dirt road in the country. Dark was coming, and it had rained all day. He was so sick, vet said he had a bad fever, he's better now and I can tell we will be just as close as I was with my old buddy. Named him Lucky, seemed appropriate since he was missed being ran over by all the  cars, trucks on that road.

Animals are the closest thing to angels on earth. They are pure and will just return the love you give them.

Some die from the neck bite which the male uses to keep the female in place. Some cats die during giving birth because the babies are 3-4 times larger than domestic kittens. A F1 savannah it will cost you up to $25K because it parents are a serval and a domestic cat. 
Now a F2 savannah will cost less because it's parents are an F2 savannah and a domestic cat.
And an F3 savannah will cost less the an F2, why? Because it's parents are an F2 savannah and a domestic cat, you are getting further away from the serval cat breed and the wildness that's in the F1 savannah cat.

video_description: visit f1savannahkittens.com to learn about our kittens.
text_content:
I have a serval. She has a very large room and an enclosure. She is happy and she sleeps under my covers with me. The most important thing is to always respect her and in turn, she will love you. 
I also don’t play with her a certain way due to her claws. She is a great companion and has been my therapy cat for 5 years. 
I cannot work so I spend all day and night with her. 

Servals are wild animals and this must be kept in consideration. Definitely do not get a serval if your job could move you out of state and if you have kids. These cats get stressed easily and they can die. I waited 20 years until I settled down to buy one. And never plan to take a vacation ever again. No one is equipped with handling her.

I was raised in the 60s with an Iriomote cat my parents rescued while still nursing. Their house cat nursed till it could eat on its own. She was 16 when I was born and passed away when I was 6. I took responsibility for rescue when I was 7. A Jaguar was found dead in a trap with a black Jaguar kitten close. Barely alive I nursed it to health. My mother taught their cat to use a toilet so I naturally did the same. We had a good laugh in the 70s when people sold training kits on TV. We were very remote so I pushed her outdoors. I took her with me when I hunted for her food. She started taking off on her own but never gave up her spot on my bed or in my room. She went absolutely wild when I  had girls over. When I was 19, I had a girl over, and you would have thought she raised her. That same day, after the three of us spent the day up on a mountain,  my friend sat on the couch, and the cat was curled up next to her. I was in the kitchen when my mom got home. I immediately asked my mom if she wanted to meet my future wife. I SAID she doesn't know it yet, but go look on the couch. My mother looked at me and said, I don't think she has a choice
 We did get married, and the cat was her shadow. We just celebrated our 49th wedding anniversary. When our son was born, you would have thought he was a cat's child. It was like a full circle of life when the cat died when our son was 5.

text_content:
I had an F2 Savannah. Lovely boy. Very good guard dog too. He was huge and quite beefy. Like a beefy greyhound. A box of 5 of them was dumped on my driveway. I think they thought it was a mis-mating, sadly a lot of dodgy breeders where we lived.

I'd already climbed about 10 foot up the side of a cliff to recuse two kittens someone had thrown off the top. Tide was out at the time. They were bengals with belly button hernias. You're not supposed to continue breeding from a queen if you see this happen as it's a hereditary condition. But like I said dodgy breeders!!! So being the countryside and everyone knowing everyone, I must be insane. I just had to make sure they didn't strangle the hernias and both girls were neutered on a midline incision rather than on the side and hernia popped back in. Then rehomed
one of them.  

 Well I took the box of kittens to the vet to check they were okay. She had the genetic markers tested and it must have been a bengal that got at the Queen as these were F2 savannahs. 

We then had to call everyone who we'd rehired a kitten to and tell them to insure their cats for 10k and don't let them outside on their own if they were friendly. They also had to have them neutered.

I kept the cheekiest. He was happy as heck with us but totally German Shepard with those he didn't know. He loved going out in the forest with us on a harness and lead and we lived down a long drive so there were three huge paddocks and he'd be okay out running around those. He was also trained on command to bring his pompoms for playing fetch.

I did have to explain to a friend that he didn't know her and she was not going to enjoy it when she kept trying to play with him. And he did attack her. Bengals are bred from wild cats and cervals are wild cats so you know he had German Shepard sized paws but razor sharp. 

He was great with the other cats though. My bengal girl decided she was his mum, well for the few weeks she was bigger than him, but she still let her clean him. He also would want to know everything I was doing, including when cooking. 

And if he felt a kid was getting to close to my child he was big enough to put that child to the ground. My child would say don't move, he wouldn't hurt them, and I'd then call him and he'd go back to happy cat again and come to me. 

When we went out for walks he wasn't territorial and would let strangers pet him. Postman was scare sh*tless of him though!

video_title: The Serval Cat Traits and Pet Considerations 😍
text_content: Everyone needs to notice every single home in which these beautiful cats are pets. They are owned by people with means to be able to afford these cats. They are very expensive, and very hard to obtain. You need to own a large home to give them room to roam. It would be a great idea to own land too. I can’t even afford fresh quality meat at times. And you have to buy these kitties meats with no additives, no hormones. As, the big bags of like frozen chicken you find in groceries stores can make them very sick( can us too!!) So, for us regular folks, let’s just enjoy the videos of those that do have them! God bless everyone!!!

video_title: The Serval Cat Traits and Pet Considerations 😍
text_content: Oh, I forgot to mention, a pure Serval is illegal in most states. But you can get a Savannah kitty, that is part serval. They are beautiful, wonder Al kitties. Their price?? $15,000–$40,000, according to what class it is.

text_content: Beautiful but the fact he only eats raw meat, is too expensive. Servals are wild African cats with sharp teeth and hooked claws that are native to the African savannah. They are considered dangerous animals and some say they are not good pets. The Born Free Foundation advises against keeping servals as pets and calls for stronger legislation to protect both animals and people. The Wildheart Trust also launched the SERVIVAL campaign to ban the breeding of exotic felids with domestic cats in the UK.

text_content: To those unfamiliar with Servals: Servals communicate very differently than all other cats. Hissing for regular cats indicate aggression, fear while Servals hiss  for so many other non aggressive reasons including excitement, happiness or just letting you know who knows what. Chloe is not hissing because she is wild but because she is specifically a serval. I can see why tho that would unnerve some. I have a hybrid Savannah who does same ( not as frequently) — it’s just regular communication to them— 😂

That is something a lot of people seem to do with any animal. They don’t wanna give it the proper mental stimulation or interaction. My own mother is heavily guilty of this with her regular 13 to 12 cats and three dogs.

text_content: You notice how she said the white tiger was owned by a famous “young boy” rapper in Louisiana who was abusing her and the authorities saw it because it was posted on social media. NBA Youngboy is a rapper from Louisiana and he owned a white tiger in 2018 that was seized from him due to what authorities saw from his posts on social media. They blurred his face but I could still tell it was him, plus she obviously wanted people to know who he was by calling him a young boy rapper knowing his stage name is NBA Youngboy.  I don’t  remember the tigers being seized because of abuse. I heard that they were seized because private ownership of tigers is illegal in Louisiana.  Not saying abuse didn’t occur. I just don’t remember that being the reason the tigers were seized.

video_title: Private Tour of America’s Secret Big Cat Rescue
video_description: This is America's secret big cat rescue center. Thank you to Lions Tigers & Bears for having me!
text_content: LT&B is an amazing place. I'm a member there and go on a fairly regular basis. You can tell how well the animals are cared for and I love how much space they all have to roam. For those asking - you can absolutely visit this place for a tour and they even have an Air BnB on site, close to Nola and Moka's enclosure - that you can stay the night in. You can pay extra to help feed an animal of your choice (with poles - through the no-contact guidelines) and then explore on your own after the tour. All of the volunteers that I've met are great as well and they love it there. I'm on the waitlist to volunteer myself.

I can enjoy watching a video when someone lowers their voice to talk quietly, but not when they either put a microphone right up to their mouth or worse yet, try to whisper which comes across as a loud hissing sound. Ruins the experience for me.

video_title: Rank These Animals To Have As A Pet! #shorts #animals #ranking #pet #tiger
text_content:
Remember, you gotta feed it and look after it. And there's the risk it can attack others (huge lawsuit/prison) or attack you instead (disabled/dead).

For those reasons:
1- Flying Squirrel (pretty cute, somewhat cool, not dangerous or expensive to look after)
2- Hermit Crab (even cheaper and easier to own)
3- Ostrich (okay, you will need a farm. Kinda dangerous but manageable)
4- Tiger (no way. You will basically become a zoo keeper)
5- Gorilla (cheaper and less dangerous than a Tiger, but note in many places Gorilla's have rights similar to Humans. So their care and habitat is to a much higher standard, meaning you have to be extremely wealthy to even consider this. Much more so than a Tiger).

text_content:
Fun fact! Cats and lions are actually in two completely different genuses of cats that diverged from each other 6-10 million years ago. 

Lions are part of the Panthera group which includes Tigers, Leopards, Snow Leopards and Jaguars. House cats are part of the Felinae group and thus are much more closely related to Cheetahs, Lynxes, Cougars  and Ocelots, plus a whole bunch of lesser known cats like the Carcal, Fishing Cat, the Sand Cat and the Rusty-Spotted Cat

text_content:
The fact that's she's still eating and playful is promising, so if they're legitimately thinking it truly is cancer and (possibly you can request a full body scan) the cancer hasn't spread throughout, please go through with the amputation to hopefully prevent further spread.  Animals are SO resilient and can do surprisingly well on 3 legs. We have several "tripod" cats and dogs in the area who are thriving.  Have they recommended any supplements for joints that can hopefully alleviate some of the extra strain that will be on the remaining legs post amputation?  They'll be taking on the extra 25% of what was lost with the 4th leg. 

Wishing her (and you guys) the best.  I was told my rescue cat would never run after having both back legs broken and then dragging them around for months outside. After I adopted him, I did a high protein diet and PT twice per day, and he was running within months. He even does a cat wheel now. Animals are so resilient. Wishing all of you the best. ❤

text_content:
I have an 18 year old british shorthair, who also had her back right leg amputated. Was about 2-3 years ago. She was really weak and the vet advised us to put her down, but because they were a friend of ours, they agreed to do the amputation. Just months later our cat was sprinting around the house and climbing furniture, like she lost an additional 5 years along with her leg and she's still going strong!

Point is, cat are fighters and very resilient, so I'm feeling very optimistic for Chloe! Still though, I wish you the best of luck and that the surgery will go smoothly!
